author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew IX. 18.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “While He spake these things unto them, behold, there came ina ruler, and worshipped Him, saying, My daughter is even now dead; but come and lay Thy hand upon her, and she shall live.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The deed overtook the words; so that the mouths of the Pharisees were the more stopped.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both he that came was a ruler of the synagogue, and his affliction terrible.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the young damsel was both his only child, and twelve years old, the very flower of her age; on which account especially He raised her up again, and that immediately.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if Luke say that men came, saying, “Trouble not the Master, for she is dead;”we will say this, that the expression, “she is even now dead,” was that of one conjecturing from the time of his journeying, or exaggerating his affliction.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is an usual thing with persons in need to heighten their own evils by their report, and to say something more than is really true, the more to attract those whom they are beseeching.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But see his dullness: how he requires of Christ two things, both His actual presence, and the laying on of His hand: and this by the way is a sign that he had left her still breathing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This Naaman also, that Syrian, required of the prophet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For I thought,” saith he, “he will surely come out, and will lay on his hand.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in truth they who are more or less dull of temper, require sight and sensible things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas Marksaith, He took the three disciples, and so doth Luke;our evangelist merely saith, “the disciples.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then did He not take with Him Matthew, though he had but just come unto Him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To bring him to a more earnest longing, and because he was yet rather in an imperfect state.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to this intent doth He honor those, that these may grow such as those are.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But for him it sufficed for the present, to see what befell the woman with the issue of blood, and to be honored by His table, and by His partaking of his salt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when He had risen up many followed Him, as for a great miracle, both on account of the person who had come, and because the more part being of a grosser disposition were seeking not so much the care of the soul, as the healing of the body; and they flowed together, some urged by their own afflictions, some hastening to behold how other men’s were cured: however, there were as yet but few in the habit of coming principally for the sake of His words and doctrine.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, He did not suffer them to enter into the house, but His disciples only; and not even all of these, everywhere instructing us to repel the applause of the multitude.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And, behold,” it is said, “a woman that had an issue of blood twelve years, came behind Him, and touched the hem of His garment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For she said within herself, If I may but touch His garment, I shall be whole.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore did she not approach Him boldly?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: She was ashamed on account of her affliction, accounting herself to be unclean.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the menstruous woman was judged not to be clean, much more would she have the same thought, who was afflicted with such a disease; since in fact that complaint was under the law accounted a great uncleanness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore she lies hidden, and conceals herself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither had she as yet the proper and correct opinion concerning Him: else she would not have thought to be concealed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this is the first woman that came unto Him in public, having heard of course that He heals women also, and that He is on His way to the little daughter that was dead.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And she durst not invite Him to her house, although she was wealthy;nay, neither did she approach publicly, but secretly with faith she touched His garments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For she did not doubt, nor say in herself, “Shall I indeed be delivered from the disease? shall I indeed fail of deliverance?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But confident of her health, she so approached Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For she said,” we read, “in herself, If I may only touch His garment, I shall be whole.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for she saw out of what manner of house He was come, that of the publicans, and who they were that followed Him, sinners and publicans; and all these things made her to be of good hope.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then doth Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He suffers her not to be hid, but brings her into the midst, and makes her manifest for many purposes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is true indeed that some of the senseless ones say, “He does this for love of glory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For why,” say they, “did He not suffer her to be hid?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What sayest thou, unholy, yea, all unholy one?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He that enjoins silence, He that passes by miracles innumerable, is He in love with glory?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what intent then doth He bring her forward?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the first place He puts an end to the woman’s fear, lest being pricked by her conscience, as having stolen the gift, she should abide in agony.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the second place, He sets her right, in respect of her thinking to be hid.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thirdly, He exhibits her faith to all, so as to provoke the rest also to emulation; and His staying of the fountains of her blood was no greater sign than He affords in signifying His knowledge of all things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Moreover the ruler of the synagogue, who was on the point of thorough unbelief, and so of utter ruin, He corrects by the woman.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since both they that came said, “Trouble not the Master, for the damsel is dead;” and those in the house laughed Him to scorn, when He said, “She sleepeth;” and it was likely that the father too should have experienced some such feeling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore to correct this weakness beforehand, He brings forward the simple woman.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to that ruler being quite of the grosser sort, hear what He saith unto him: “Fear not, do thou believe only, and she shall be made whole.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus He waited also on purpose for death to come on, and that then He should arrive; in order that the proof of the resurrection might be distinct.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With this view He both walks more leisurely, and discourses more with the woman; that He might give time for the damsel to die, and for those to come, who told of it, and said, “Trouble not the Master.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This again surely the evangelist obscurely signifies, when he saith, “While He yet spake, there came from the house certain which said, Thy daughter is dead, trouble not the Master.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For His will was that her death should be believed, that her resurrection might not be suspected.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He doth in every instance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So also in the case of Lazarus, He waited a first and a second and a third day.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On account then of all these things He brings her forward, and saith, “Daughter, be of good cheer,”even as He had said also to the paralyzed person, “Son, be of good cheer.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because in truth the woman was exceedingly alarmed; therefore He saith, “be of good cheer,” and He calls her “daughter;” for her faith had made her a daughter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After that comes also her praise: “Thy faith hath made thee whole.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Luke tells us also other things more than these concerning the woman.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, when she had approached Him, saith he, and had received her health, Christ did not immediately call her, but first He saith, “Which is he that touched me?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then when Peter and they that were with Him said, Master, the multitude throng Thee, and press Thee, and sayest Thou, who touched me?”(which was a very sure sign both that He was encompassed with real flesh, and that He trampled on all vainglory, for they did not follow Him at all afar off, but thronged Him on every side); He for His part continued to say, “Somebody hath touched me, for I perceive that virtue is gone out of me;”answering after a grosser manner according to the impression of His hearers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things He said, that He might also induce her of herself to make confession.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For on this account neither did He immediately convict her, in order that having signified that He knows all things clearly, He might induce her of her own accord to publish all, and work upon her to proclaim herself what had been done, and that He might not incur suspicion by saying it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the woman superior to the ruler of the synagogue?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: She detained Him not, she took no hold of Him, but touched Him only with the end of her fingers, and though she came later, she first went away healed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And he indeed was bringing the Physician altogether to his house, but for her a mere touch suffered.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though she was bound by her affliction, yet her faith had given her wings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And mark how He comforts her, saying, “Thy faith hath saved thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now surely, had He drawn her forward for display, He would not have added this; but He saith this, partly teaching the ruler of the synagogue to believe, partly proclaiming the woman’s praise, and affording her by these words delight and advantage equal to her bodily health.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that He did this as minded to glorify her, and to amend others, and not to show Himself glorious, is manifest from hence; that He indeed would have been equally an object of admiration even without this (for the miracles were pouring around Him faster than the snow-flakes, and He both had done and was to do far greater things than these): but the woman, had this not happened, would have gone away hid, deprived of those great praises.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause He brought her forward, and proclaimed her praise, and cast out her fear, (for “she came,” it is said, “trembling”); and He caused her to be of good courage, and together with health of body, He gave her also other provisions for her journey, in that He said, “Go in peace.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And when He came into the ruler’s house, and saw the minstrels and the people making a noise, He saith unto them, Give place, for the maid is not dead, but sleepeth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they laughed Him to scorn.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Noble tokens, surely, these, of the rulers of synagogues; in the moment of her death pipes and cymbals raising a dirge!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then doth Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All the rest He cast out, but the parents He brought in; to leave no room for saying that He healed her in any other way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And before her resurrection too, He raises her in His word; saying, “The maid is not dead, but sleepeth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in many instances besides He doeth this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As then on the sea He expels tumult from the mind of the by-standers, at the same time both signifying that it is easy for Him to raise the dead (which same thing He did with respect to Lazarus also, saying, “Our friend Lazarus sleepeth;” and also teaching us not to fear death; for that it is not death, but is henceforth become a sleep.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, since He Himself was to die, He doth in the persons of others prepare His disciples beforehand to be of good courage, and to bear the end meekly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since in truth, when He had come, death was from that time forward a sleep.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But yet they laughed Him to scorn: He however was not indignant at being disbelieved by those for whom He was a little afterwards to work miracles; neither did He rebuke their laughter, in order that both it and the pipes, and the cymbals, and all the other things, might be a sure proof of her death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since for the most part, after the miracles are done, men disbelieve, He takes them beforehand by their own answers; which was done in the case both of Lazarus and of Moses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to Moses first He saith, “What is that in thine hand?”in order that when he saw it become a serpent, He should not forget that it was a rod before, but being reminded of his own saying, might be amazed at what was done.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And with regard to Lazarus He saith, “Where have ye laid him?”that they who had said, “Come and see,” and “he stinketh, for he hath been dead four days,” might no longer be able to disbelieve His having raised a dead man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seeing then the cymbals and the multitude, He put them all out, and in the presence of the parents works the miracle; not introducing another soul, but recalling the same that had gone out, and awakening her as it were out of a sleep.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He holds her by the hand, assuring the beholders; so as by that sight to make a way for the belief of her resurrection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas the father said, “Lay thy hand upon her;” He on His part doth somewhat more, for He lays no hand on her, but rather takes hold of her, and raises her, implying that to Him all things are ready.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He not only raises her up, but also commands to give her meat, that the event might not seem to be an illusion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He doth not give it Himself, but commands them; as also with regard to Lazarus He said, “Loose him, and let him go,”and afterwards makes him partaker of His table.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so is He wont always to establish both points, making out with all completeness the demonstration alike of the death and of the resurrection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do thou mark, I pray thee, not her resurrection only, but also His commanding “to tell no man;” and by all learn thou this especially, His freedom from haughtiness and vainglory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And withal learn this other thing also, that He cast them that were beating themselves out of the house, and declared them unworthy of such a sight; and do not thou go out with the minstrels, but remain with Peter, and John, and James.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if He cast them out then, much more now.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For then it was not yet manifest that death was become a sleep, but now this is clearer than the very sun itself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But is it that He hath not raised thy daughter now?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But surely He will raise her, and with more abundant glory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that damsel, when she had risen, died again; but thy child, if she rise again, abides thenceforth in immortal being.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let no man therefore beat himself any more, nor wail, neither disparage Christ’s achievement.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed He overcame death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then dost thou wail for nought?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The thing is become a sleep.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why lament and weep?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, even if Greeksdid this, they should be laughed to scorn; but when the believer behaves himself unseemly in these things, what plea hath he?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What excuse will there be for them that are guilty of such folly, and this, after so long a time, and so clear proof of the resurrection?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou, as though laboring to add to the charge against thee, dost also bring us in heathen women singing dirges, to kindle thy feelings, and to stir up the furnace thoroughly: and thou hearkenest not to Paul, saying, “What concord hath Christ with Belial? or what part hath he that believeth with an infidel?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And while the children of heathens, who know nothing of resurrection, do yet find words of consolation, saying, “Bear it manfully, for it is not possible to undo what hath taken place, nor to amend it by lamentations;” art not thou, who hearest sayings wiser and better than these, ashamed to behave thyself more unseemly than they?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For we say not at all, “Bear it manfully, because it is not possible to undo what hath taken place,” but, “bear it manfully, because he will surely rise again;” the child sleeps and is not dead; he is at rest and hath not perished.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For resurrection will be his final lot, and eternal life, and immortality, and an angel’s portion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearest thou not the Psalm that saith, “Return unto thy rest, O my soul, for the Lord hath dealt bountifully with thee?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: God calleth it “bountiful dealing,” and dost thou make lamentation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what more couldest thou have done, if thou wert a foe and an enemy of the dead?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, if there must be mourning, it is the devil that ought to mourn.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He may beat himself, he may wail, at our journeying to greater blessings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This lamentation becomes his wickedness, not thee, who art going to be crowned and to rest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for death is a fair haven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Consider, at any rate, with how many evils our present life is filled; reflect how often thou thyself hast cursed our present life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed things go on to worse, and from the very beginning thou wert involved in no small condemnation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, saith He, “In sorrow thou shalt bring forth children;” and, “In the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat thy bread;”and, “In the world ye shall have tribulation.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But of our state there, no such word at all is spoken, but all the contrary; that “grief and sorrow and sighing have fled away.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that “men shall come from the east and from the west, and shall recline in the bosoms of Abraham and Isaac and Jacob.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that the region there is a spiritual bride-chamber, and bright lamps, and a translation to Heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then disgrace the departed?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why dispose the rest to fear and tremble at death?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why cause many to accuse God, as though He had done very dreadful things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, why after this invite poor persons, and entreat priests to pray?“In order,” saith he, “that the dead may depart into rest; that he may find the Judge propitious.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these things then art thou mourning and wailing?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou art therefore fighting and warring with thyself: exciting a storm against thyself on account of his having entered into harbor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But what can I do?” saith he: “such a thing is nature.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The blame is not nature’s, neither doth it belong to the necessary consequence of the thing; but it is we that are turning all things upside down, are overcome with softness, are giving up our proper nobility, and are making the unbelievers worse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For how shall we reason with another concerning immortality? how shall we persuade the heathen, when we fear death, and shudder at it more than he?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Many, for instance, among the Greeksalthough they knew nothing of course about immortality, have crowned themselves at the decrease of their children, and appeared in white garments, that they might reap the present glory; but thou not even for the future glory’s sake ceasest thy woman’s behavior and wailing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But hast thou no heirs, nor any to succeed to thy goods?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And which wouldest thou rather, that he should be heir of thy possessions, or of Heaven?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And which didst thou desire, that he should succeed to the things that perish, which he must have let go soon after, or to things that remain, and are immoveable?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou hadst him not for heir, but God had him instead of thee; he became not joint-heir with his own brethren, but he became “joint-heir with Christ.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But to whom,” saith he, “are we to leave our garments, to whom our houses, to whom our slaves and our lands?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To him again, and more securely than if he lived; for there is nothing to hinder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if barbarians burn the goods of the departed together with them, much more were it a righteous thing for thee to send away with the dead what things he hath: not to be turned to ashes, like those, but to invest him with more glory; and that if he departed a sinner, it may do away his sins;but if righteous, that it may become an increase of reward and recompense.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But dost thou long to see him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then live the same life with him, and thou wilt soon obtain that sacred vision.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And herewith consider this also, that though thou shouldest not hearken to us, thou wilt certainly yield to time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But no reward then for thee; for the consolation comes of the number of the days.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas if thou art willing now to command thyself, thou wilt gain two very great points: first, thou wilt deliver thyself from the intervening ills, next, thou wilt be crowned with the brighter crown from God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed neither almsgiving nor anything else is nearly so great as bearing affliction meekly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Bear in mind, that even the Son of God died: and He indeed for thee, but thou for thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when He said, “If it be possible, let the cup pass from me,”and suffered pain, and was in agony, nevertheless He shunned not the end, but underwent it, and that with its whole course of exceeding woe.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, He did by no means simply endure death, but the most shameful death; and before His death, stripes; and before His stripes, upbraidings, and jeers, and revilings; instructing thee to bear all manfully.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And though He died, and put off His body, He resumed it again in greater glory, herein also holding out to thee good hopes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If these things be not a fable, lament not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou account these things to be sure, weep not; but if thou dost weep, how wilt thou be able to persuade the Greek that thou believest?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But even so doth the event still appear intolerable to thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Well then, for this very cause it is not meet to lament for him, for he is delivered from many such calamities.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Grudge not therefore against him, neither envy him: for to ask death for yourself because of his premature end, and to lament for him that he did not live to endure many such things, is rather the part of one grudging and envying.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And think not of this, that he will no more return home: but that thyself also art a little while after to go to him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Regard not this, that he returns here no more, but that neither do these things that are seen remain such as they are, but these too are being transformed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for heaven, and earth, and sea, and all, are being put together afresh,and then shalt thou recover thy child in greater glory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if indeed he departed a sinner, his wickedness is stayed; for certainly, had God known that he was being converted, He would not have snatched him away before his repentance: but if he ended his life righteous, he now possesses all good in safety.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence it is manifest that thy tears are not of kindly affection, but of unreasoning passion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou lovedst the departed, thou shouldest rejoice and be glad that he is delivered from the present waves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what is there more, I pray thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What is there fresh and new?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do we not see the same things daily revolving?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Day and night, night and day, winter and summer, summer and winter, and nothing more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these indeed are ever the same; but our evils are fresh, and newer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wouldest thou then have him every day drawing up more of these things, and abiding here, and sickening, and mourning, and in fear and trembling, and enduring some of the ills of life, dreading others lest he some time endure them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since assuredly thou canst not say this, that one sailing over this great sea might possibly be free from despondency and cares, and from all other such things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And withal take this also into account, that thou didst not bring him forth immortal; and that if he had not died now, he must have endured it soon after.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But is it that thou hadst not thy fill of him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou wilt of a certainty enjoy him there.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But longest thou to see him here also?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is there to hinder thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thou art permitted even here, if thou be watchful; for the hope of the things to come is clearer than sight.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou, if he were in some king’s court wouldest not ever seek to see him, so long as thou heardest of his good report: and seeing him departed to the things that are far better, art thou faint-hearted about a little time; and that, when thou hast in his place one to dwell with thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But hast thou no husband? yet hast thou a consolation, even the Father of the orphans, and Judge of the widows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear even Paul pronouncing this widowhood blessed, and saying, “Now she that is a widow indeed and desolate, trusteth in the Lord.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because such an one will appear more approved, evincing as she doth greater patience.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Mourn not therefore for that which is thy crown, that for which thou demandest a reward.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since thou hast also restored His deposit, if thou hast exhibited the very thing entrusted to thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Be not in care any more, having laid up the possession in an inviolable treasure-house.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou wouldest really learn, both what is our present being, and what our life to come; and that the one is a spider’s web and a shadow, but the things there, all of them, immoveable and immortal; thou wouldest not after that want other arguments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas now thy child is delivered from all change; if he were here, perhaps he might continue good, perhaps not so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not how many openly cast offtheir own children? how many are constrained to keep them at home, although worse than the open outcasts?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us make account of all these things and practise self-command; for so shall we at once show regard to the deceased, and enjoy much praise from men, and receive from God the great rewards of patience, and attain unto the good things eternal; unto which may we all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might forever and ever.
